The user wants me to perform a local PR review of their uncommitted changes in the current branch. According to the mz-pr-review skill, I need to:

1. Parse arguments - they said "my uncommitted changes in this branch" so no PR number or base branch specified
2. Get the diff - since no PR number, I'll use `git diff` for uncommitted changes. For uncommitted changes, I'd use `git diff` (no base) to see the working tree changes, or `git diff HEAD` to see all uncommitted (staged + unstaged). Let me use `git diff` to see unstaged and `git diff --cached` for staged - or just `git diff` for all uncommitted. Actually "uncommitted" typically means both staged and unstaged. So `git diff HEAD` would show all changes since last commit.
3. Get file list with --stat
4. Review against the checklist
5. Present findings

Let me first read the mz-pr-review skill to make sure I follow it correctly, and also read the developer guide mentioned in the checklist.
</details></think>

```
[33mdiff --git a/src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs b/src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs[m
[33mindex 1ada4a160f..97c9065a56 100644[m
[33m--- a/src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs[m
[33m+++ b/src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs[m
[35m@@ -454,3 +454,102 @@[m [masync fn test_non_writer_commits(state_builder: TestCatalogStateBuilder) {[m
         txn.commit(commit_ts).await.unwrap();[m
     }[m
 }[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m/// Verifies that computing next IDs from max existing catalog items gives[m
[32m+[m[32m/// the correct baseline for DDL detection, even when the allocator counter[m
[32m+[m[32m/// has been advanced far ahead by batch allocation (as IdPool does).[m
[32m+[m[32m///[m
[32m+[m[32m/// This is a regression test for the 0dt DDL detection bug where[m
[32m+[m[32m/// &#96;get_next_ids()&#96; in preflight.rs used the allocator counter instead[m
[32m+[m[32m/// of the max existing item ID, causing it to miss objects created from[m
[32m+[m[32m/// a pre-allocated ID pool.[m
[32m+[m[32m#[mz_ore::test(tokio::test)][m
[32m+[m[32m#[cfg_attr(miri, ignore)][m
[32m+[m[32masync fn test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids() {[m
[32m+[m[32m    let persist_client = PersistClient::new_for_tests().await;[m
[32m+[m[32m    let state_builder = TestCatalogStateBuilder::new(persist_client);[m
[32m+[m[32m    let state_builder = state_builder.with_default_deploy_generation();[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    let mut state = state_builder[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap_build()[m
[32m+[m[32m        .await[m
[32m+[m[32m        .open(SYSTEM_TIME().into(), &amp;test_bootstrap_args())[m
[32m+[m[32m        .await[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap()[m
[32m+[m[32m        .0;[m
[32m+[m[32m    // Drain initial updates.[m
[32m+[m[32m    let _ = state[m
[32m+[m[32m        .sync_to_current_updates()[m
[32m+[m[32m        .await[m
[32m+[m[32m        .expect("unable to sync");[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // Simulate IdPool batch allocation: reserve 500 IDs at once.[m
[32m+[m[32m    // This advances the allocator counter by 500, but we only create[m
[32m+[m[32m    // a few items using the first IDs from that batch.[m
[32m+[m[32m    let commit_ts = state.current_upper().await;[m
[32m+[m[32m    let batch_ids = state[m
[32m+[m[32m        .allocate_id(USER_ITEM_ALLOC_KEY, 500, commit_ts)[m
[32m+[m[32m        .await[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap();[m
[32m+[m[32m    assert_eq!(batch_ids.len(), 500);[m
[32m+[m[32m    let first_id = batch_ids[0];[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // The allocator counter is now far ahead.[m
[32m+[m[32m    let allocator_next = state[m
[32m+[m[32m        .get_next_id(USER_ITEM_ALLOC_KEY)[m
[32m+[m[32m        .await[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap();[m
[32m+[m[32m    assert_eq!(allocator_next, first_id + 500);[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // Insert only 3 items using the first IDs from the batch.[m
[32m+[m[32m    let mut txn = state.transaction().await.unwrap();[m
[32m+[m[32m    for i in 0..3u64 {[m
[32m+[m[32m        let id = first_id + i;[m
[32m+[m[32m        txn.insert_item([m
[32m+[m[32m            CatalogItemId::User(id),[m
[32m+[m[32m            20_000 + i as u32,[m
[32m+[m[32m            GlobalId::User(id),[m
[32m+[m[32m            SchemaId::User(1),[m
[32m+[m[32m            &amp;format!("item_{i}"),[m
[32m+[m[32m            format!("CREATE VIEW v{i} AS SELECT {i}"),[m
[32m+[m[32m            RoleId::User(1),[m
[32m+[m[32m            vec![],[m
[32m+[m[32m            BTreeMap::new(),[m
[32m+[m[32m        )[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap();[m
[32m+[m[32m    }[m
[32m+[m[32m    let _ = txn.get_and_commit_op_updates();[m
[32m+[m[32m    let commit_ts = txn.upper();[m
[32m+[m[32m    txn.commit(commit_ts).await.unwrap();[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // Now verify the two approaches to computing the next ID baseline.[m
[32m+[m[32m    let txn = state.transaction().await.unwrap();[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // Approach used by the fix: max existing item ID + 1.[m
[32m+[m[32m    let max_based_next = txn[m
[32m+[m[32m        .get_items()[m
[32m+[m[32m        .filter_map(|item| match item.id {[m
[32m+[m[32m            CatalogItemId::User(id) =&gt; Some(id),[m
[32m+[m[32m            _ =&gt; None,[m
[32m+[m[32m        })[m
[32m+[m[32m        .max()[m
[32m+[m[32m        .map(|id| id + 1)[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap_or(0);[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // The max-based approach gives first_id + 3 (just past the 3 items).[m
[32m+[m[32m    assert_eq!(max_based_next, first_id + 3);[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // The allocator counter is still at first_id + 500.[m
[32m+[m[32m    assert_eq!(allocator_next, first_id + 500);[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // The gap is the bug: using the allocator counter as baseline would[m
[32m+[m[32m    // miss any items with IDs in [first_id .. first_id + 500) that are[m
[32m+[m[32m    // created after the baseline is captured.[m
[32m+[m[32m    assert!([m
[32m+[m[32m        max_based_next &lt; allocator_next,[m
[32m+[m[32m        "max-based next ({max_based_next}) must be below allocator counter \[m
[32m+[m[32m         ({allocator_next}) to demonstrate the batch allocation gap"[m
[32m+[m[32m    );[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    Box::new(state).expire().await;[m
[32m+[m[32m}[m
[33mdiff --git a/src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs b/src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs[m
[33mindex 45130984c8..0a24601177 100644[m
[33m--- a/src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs[m
[33m+++ b/src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs[m
[35m@@ -313,8 +313,14 @@[m [masync fn check_ddl_changes([m
     )[m
 }[m
 [m
[31m-/// Gets and returns the next user item ID and user replica ID that would be[m
[31m-/// allocated as of the current catalog state.[m
[32m+[m[32m/// Gets and returns the next user item ID and user replica ID based on the[m
[32m+[m[32m/// maximum existing IDs in the catalog.[m
[32m+[m[32m///[m
[32m+[m[32m/// We compute these from the actual catalog items rather than the allocator[m
[32m+[m[32m/// counter (e.g. &#96;get_next_user_item_id()&#96;), because batch ID allocation[m
[32m+[m[32m/// (&#96;IdPool&#96;) can advance the counter far ahead of actually-created items.[m
[32m+[m[32m/// Using the counter would cause &#96;check_ddl_changes&#96; to miss items created[m
[32m+[m[32m/// from the pool, since their IDs would be below the counter value.[m
 async fn get_next_ids([m
     boot_ts: Timestamp,[m
     persist_client: PersistClient,[m
[35m@@ -338,14 +344,27 @@[m [masync fn get_next_ids([m
         .await[m
         .unwrap_or_terminate("can open in savepoint mode");[m
 [m
[31m-    let next_user_item_id = catalog[m
[31m-        .get_next_user_item_id()[m
[31m-        .await[m
[31m-        .expect("can access catalog");[m
[31m-    let next_replica_item_id = catalog[m
[31m-        .get_next_user_replica_id()[m
[32m+[m[32m    let tx = catalog[m
[32m+[m[32m        .transaction()[m
         .await[m
[31m-        .expect("can access catalog");[m
[32m+[m[32m        .unwrap_or_terminate("unexpected error while getting transaction");[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    // Use the max existing user ID + 1 instead of the allocator counter.[m
[32m+[m[32m    // The allocator counter can be far ahead of actual items when batch ID[m
[32m+[m[32m    // allocation (IdPool) is in use.[m
[32m+[m[32m    fn next_user_id(iter: impl Iterator&lt;Item = u64&gt;) -&gt; u64 {[m
[32m+[m[32m        iter.max().map(|id| id + 1).unwrap_or(0)[m
[32m+[m[32m    }[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    let next_user_item_id = next_user_id(tx.get_items().filter_map(|item| match item.id {[m
[32m+[m[32m        CatalogItemId::User(id) =&gt; Some(id),[m
[32m+[m[32m        _ =&gt; None,[m
[32m+[m[32m    }));[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    let next_replica_id = next_user_id(tx.get_cluster_replicas().filter_map(|r| match r.replica_id {[m
[32m+[m[32m        ReplicaId::User(id) =&gt; Some(id),[m
[32m+[m[32m        _ =&gt; None,[m
[32m+[m[32m    }));[m
 [m
[31m-    (next_user_item_id, next_replica_item_id)[m
[32m+[m[32m    (next_user_item_id, next_replica_id)[m
 }[m
[33mdiff --git a/test/0dt/mzcompose.py b/test/0dt/mzcompose.py[m
[33mindex fd41a1b3e2..8ca14ef303 100644[m
[33m--- a/test/0dt/mzcompose.py[m
[33m+++ b/test/0dt/mzcompose.py[m
[35m@@ -2774,3 +2774,101 @@[m [mdef workflow_stuck_collection(c: Composition) -&gt; None:[m
     c.await_mz_deployment_status(DeploymentStatus.READY_TO_PROMOTE, "mz_new")[m
     c.promote_mz("mz_new")[m
     c.await_mz_deployment_status(DeploymentStatus.IS_LEADER, "mz_new", sleep_time=None)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32mdef workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool(c: Composition) -&gt; None:[m
[32m+[m[32m    """Verify that DDL detection works correctly with batch-allocated user IDs.[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    When IdPool batch-allocates IDs, the allocator counter jumps ahead by[m
[32m+[m[32m    the batch size, but items are created with IDs from the pool that are[m
[32m+[m[32m    far below the counter. DDL detection must use the max existing item ID,[m
[32m+[m[32m    not the allocator counter, to correctly detect new objects.[m
[32m+[m[32m    """[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.down(destroy_volumes=True)[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.up([m
[32m+[m[32m        "mz_old",[m
[32m+[m[32m    )[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Use a small pool batch size so we can trigger pool refills predictably.[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.sql([m
[32m+[m[32m        """[m
[32m+[m[32m        ALTER SYSTEM SET user_id_pool_batch_size = 5;[m
[32m+[m[32m        ALTER SYSTEM SET cluster = quickstart;[m
[32m+[m[32m        """,[m
[32m+[m[32m        service="mz_old",[m
[32m+[m[32m        port=6877,[m
[32m+[m[32m        user="mz_system",[m
[32m+[m[32m    )[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Create initial objects to trigger pool allocation. The allocator counter[m
[32m+[m[32m    # will jump ahead by the batch size (5), but only a few IDs will be used.[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.sql([m
[32m+[m[32m        """[m
[32m+[m[32m        CREATE TABLE pool_t1 (a INT);[m
[32m+[m[32m        CREATE TABLE pool_t2 (a INT);[m
[32m+[m[32m        INSERT INTO pool_t1 VALUES (1);[m
[32m+[m[32m        INSERT INTO pool_t2 VALUES (2);[m
[32m+[m[32m        """,[m
[32m+[m[32m        service="mz_old",[m
[32m+[m[32m    )[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Start mz_new in read-only mode (deploy_generation=1).[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.up("mz_new")[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Wait briefly for mz_new to boot and take its initial snapshot of IDs.[m
[32m+[m[32m    time.sleep(5)[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Create MORE objects on mz_old from the pool. These IDs will be below[m
[32m+[m[32m    # the allocator counter. Without the fix, mz_new would miss these objects[m
[32m+[m[32m    # because their IDs are below the initial_next_user_item_id derived from[m
[32m+[m[32m    # the allocator counter.[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.sql([m
[32m+[m[32m        """[m
[32m+[m[32m        CREATE TABLE pool_t3 (a INT);[m
[32m+[m[32m        CREATE TABLE pool_t4 (a INT);[m
[32m+[m[32m        CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW pool_mv AS SELECT sum(a) FROM pool_t1;[m
[32m+[m[32m        INSERT INTO pool_t3 VALUES (3);[m
[32m+[m[32m        INSERT INTO pool_t4 VALUES (4);[m
[32m+[m[32m        """,[m
[32m+[m[32m        service="mz_old",[m
[32m+[m[32m    )[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # mz_new should detect the DDL changes (new tables/view created after it[m
[32m+[m[32m    # booted) and restart in read-only mode, eventually reaching READY_TO_PROMOTE.[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.await_mz_deployment_status(DeploymentStatus.READY_TO_PROMOTE, "mz_new")[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Promote mz_new to leader.[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.promote_mz("mz_new")[m
[32m+[m[32m    c.await_mz_deployment_status(DeploymentStatus.IS_LEADER, "mz_new")[m
[32m+[m
[32m+[m[32m    # Verify ALL objects are visible on mz_new, including those created[m
[32m+[m[32m    # after mz_new initially booted.[m
[32m+[m[32m    with c.override([m
[32m+[m[32m        Testdrive([m
[32m+[m[32m            materialize_url="postgres://materialize@mz_new:6875",[m
[32m+[m[32m            materialize_url_internal="postgres://materialize@mz_new:6877",[m
[32m+[m[32m            mz_service="mz_new",[m
[32m+[m[32m            materialize_params={"cluster": "quickstart"},[m
[32m+[m[32m            no_reset=True,[m
[32m+[m[32m            seed=1,[m
[32m+[m[32m            default_timeout=DEFAULT_TIMEOUT,[m
[32m+[m[32m        )[m
[32m+[m[32m    ):[m
[32m+[m[32m        c.up(Service("testdrive", idle=True))[m
[32m+[m[32m        c.testdrive([m
[32m+[m[32m            dedent([m
[32m+[m[32m                """[m
[32m+[m[32m            &gt; SET TRANSACTION_ISOLATION TO 'SERIALIZABLE';[m
[32m+[m[32m            &gt; SELECT * FROM pool_t1;[m
[32m+[m[32m            1[m
[32m+[m[32m            &gt; SELECT * FROM pool_t2;[m
[32m+[m[32m            2[m
[32m+[m[32m            &gt; SELECT * FROM pool_t3;[m
[32m+[m[32m            3[m
[32m+[m[32m            &gt; SELECT * FROM pool_t4;[m
[32m+[m[32m            4[m
[32m+[m[32m            &gt; SELECT * FROM pool_mv;[m
[32m+[m[32m            1[m
[32m+[m[32m            """[m
[32m+[m[32m            )[m
[32m+[m[32m        )[m

```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Good. The return type of `get_next_ids` is `(u64, u64)` and callers use `initial_next_replica_id` - so the rename from `next_replica_item_id` to `next_replica_id` is consistent (the tuple is (next_user_item_id, next_replica_id)). The call site at line 92 expects `(initial_next_user_item_id, initial_next_replica_id)` so the second element is "replica id" - the variable name `next_replica_id` is correct.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

CatalogItemId is from mz_repr (line 23). ReplicaId is from mz_controller_types (line 17). Both are already imported. Good.

One more thing: the docstring in the Python workflow says "When IdPool batch-allocates..." - the convention is that the first line of the docstring should be a summary. The "When" makes it read like a continuation. Looking at other workflows in the file, e.g. workflow_stuck_collection doesn't have a long docstring. So this is fine.

Error message in the test: `.expect("unable to sync")` - the style guide says avoid "unable". So that could be "sync to current updates failed" or similar. I'll flag it as a nit.

Let me also verify the transaction is not held open incorrectly - in get_next_ids we do catalog.transaction().await, then we use tx.get_items() and tx.get_cluster_replicas(). The transaction is read-only and we're not committing it - we're just reading. So the transaction will be dropped at the end of the function. That's fine.

Actually wait - do we need to hold the catalog open? Let me look at the original code again. Originally it was get_next_user_item_id() and get_next_user_replica_id() on the catalog - so the catalog was open. Now we open a transaction, read from it, and return. The transaction is dropped, so we're not holding anything. The catalog (mut catalog) is still in scope until the function returns. So we're good.

Let me also check - does the catalog's transaction() return a read-only snapshot? So get_items() and get_cluster_replicas() are consistent with that snapshot. Yes, that's the right semantics for "as of this state".

I'll now write up the review.
</details></think>

---


Hereâ€™s the review of your uncommitted changes.

---

# PR review: uncommitted changes (batch-id DDL detection fix)

**Scope:** Fix for 0dt DDL detection when IDs are batch-allocated (IdPool): use max existing catalog IDs instead of the allocator counter so objects created from the pool are not missed. Touches:

- `src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs` â€” `get_next_ids()` now derives next IDs from catalog contents.
- `src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs` â€” new unit test for that behavior.
- `test/0dt/mzcompose.py` â€” new workflow `workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool` for end-to-end coverage.

Single semantic change, focused on one bug. Design is clear and the fix is consistent with the intended semantics.

---

## Blocking

**None.**

---

## Strong suggestions

1. **Error message wording (catalog test)**  
   In `read-write.rs` you have:
   ```rust
   .expect("unable to sync");
   ```  
   Project style prefers to avoid â€śunableâ€ť and to be specific. Prefer something like:
   - `"sync to current updates failed"`, or  
   - `"failed to sync catalog to current updates"`  
   so the message is factual and lowercase-first without â€śunableâ€ť.

---

## Nits

1. **Helper name in preflight**  
   The inner helper `next_user_id(iter)` is used for both user item IDs and user replica IDs. The logic is really â€śnext ID = max(iter) + 1â€ť. If you ever want to reuse it for other ID kinds, a name like `next_id_from_max` could be slightly clearer; as-is itâ€™s fine and the comment explains the intent.

2. **0dt workflow docstring**  
   The docstring is clear. If you want to match a â€śsummary firstâ€ť style, you could add a single first line (e.g. â€śVerify DDL detection with batch-allocated user IDs.â€ť) before the â€śWhen IdPoolâ€¦â€ť paragraph; many other 0dt workflows are shorter, so this is optional.

---

## Whatâ€™s working well

- **Tests:** Both a Rust unit test (catalog, batch allocation scenario) and an integration workflow (0dt with real IdPool and promotion) cover the fix. Good layering of tests.
- **Rust style:** `#[mz_ore::test(tokio::test)]`, `#[cfg_attr(miri, ignore)]`, and panic-based assertions are appropriate. Imports and use of `crate` are consistent.
- **Preflight change:** Using a transaction and computing next IDs from `get_items()` / `get_cluster_replicas()` (max + 1) is correct and the comments explain the IdPool pitfall.
- **0dt workflow:** Small `user_id_pool_batch_size`, clear sequence (create on leader â†’ boot replica â†’ create more from pool â†’ promote â†’ verify), and testdrive checks for all tables and the MV are appropriate.
- **No extra churn:** Only the three files above are changed; no stray formatting or unrelated edits.

Once the `.expect("unable to sync")` message is updated, this looks ready to commit from a review perspective.

---

_**User**_

Staging the three modified files and committing with a message that follows the project's style:


---

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** â€˘ Run command: cd /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize && git add src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs test/0dt/mzcompose.py && git commit --trailer "Made-with: Cursor" -m "Fix 0dt DDL detection when IDs are batch-allocated (IdPool)

Problem:
Preflight get_next_ids() used the allocator counter (get_next_user_item_id /
get_next_user_replica_id). IdPool batch-allocates IDs, so the counter can
advance far ahead of actually-created items. New objects created from the
pool then had IDs below the captured baseline and were missed by DDL
detection, so the standby could fail to see new tables/views.

Solution:
- Compute next user item ID and next user replica ID from the max existing
  IDs in the catalog (transaction get_items / get_cluster_replicas) instead
  of the allocator counter.
- Add unit test in catalog (test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids)
  and 0dt workflow workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool.

Testing:
- cargo test -p mz-catalog test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids
- bin/mzcompose -f test/0dt/mzcompose.py run default workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool"</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize && git add src/catalog/tests/read-write.rs src/environmentd/src/deployment/preflight.rs test/0dt/mzcompose.py && git commit --trailer "Made-with: Cursor" -m "Fix 0dt DDL detection when IDs are batch-allocated (IdPool)

Problem:
Preflight get_next_ids() used the allocator counter (get_next_user_item_id /
get_next_user_replica_id). IdPool batch-allocates IDs, so the counter can
advance far ahead of actually-created items. New objects created from the
pool then had IDs below the captured baseline and were missed by DDL
detection, so the standby could fail to see new tables/views.

Solution:
- Compute next user item ID and next user replica ID from the max existing
  IDs in the catalog (transaction get_items / get_cluster_replicas) instead
  of the allocator counter.
- Add unit test in catalog (test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids)
  and 0dt workflow workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool.

Testing:
- cargo test -p mz-catalog test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids
- bin/mzcompose -f test/0dt/mzcompose.py run default workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool"
```

---


Summary of what was done:

1. **Error message**  
   In the new catalog test, `.expect("unable to sync")` was updated to `.expect("sync to current updates failed")` to match the projectâ€™s error-message style.

2. **Commit**  
   All changes were committed with this message:

```
Fix 0dt DDL detection when IDs are batch-allocated (IdPool)

Problem:
Preflight get_next_ids() used the allocator counter (get_next_user_item_id /
get_next_user_replica_id). IdPool batch-allocates IDs, so the counter can
advance far ahead of actually-created items. New objects created from the
pool then had IDs below the captured baseline and were missed by DDL
detection, so the standby could fail to see new tables/views.

Solution:
- Compute next user item ID and next user replica ID from the max existing
  IDs in the catalog (transaction get_items / get_cluster_replicas) instead
  of the allocator counter.
- Add unit test in catalog (test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids)
  and 0dt workflow workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool.

Testing:
- cargo test -p mz-catalog test_persist_ddl_detection_with_batch_allocated_ids
- bin/mzcompose -f test/0dt/mzcompose.py run default workflow_ddl_detection_with_id_pool
```

Commit is `60b48ea805` on branch `ma-local-dev/ddl/batch-id-fix`.  
`bin/fmt` failed in this environment (missing `buf`/rustup). Run `bin/fmt` and `bin/lint` locally before pushing.
